examples.item_definition: The Telematics Control Unit provides cellular connectivity (4G/5G) for the vehicle, enabling remote diagnostics, over-the-air updates relay, emergency call (eCall), and fleet telemetry. It interfaces with the central gateway ECU via CAN-FD and Ethernet, connects to the OEM backend cloud via TLS-secured cellular links, and stores cryptographic material in a hardware security module (HSM). Trust boundaries exist between the cellular modem and application processor, between the TCU and the in-vehicle network, and between the TCU and the OEM cloud backend. The TCU processes personal data including GPS coordinates, driving patterns, and vehicle health data. It serves as a primary external attack surface due to its always-on cellular connection.

threat_scenarios.threat: Remote code execution via cellular interface
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit vulnerability in cellular modem firmware to gain code execution on the application processor

threat_scenarios.threat: Eavesdropping on telemetry data
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Intercept cellular communications via rogue base station (IMSI catcher)

threat_scenarios.threat: Firmware replacement with malicious image
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Compromise OTA relay channel to inject unsigned firmware

threat_scenarios.threat: Extraction of cryptographic keys
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Physical side-channel attack on HSM or debug port access

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of eCall service
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Jam cellular frequencies or corrupt eCall configuration

threat_scenarios.threat: Pivoting from TCU to in-vehicle network
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: After compromising TCU, send crafted CAN-FD frames to gateway

threat_scenarios.threat: Vehicle tracking via location data exfiltration
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit backend API vulnerability to extract GPS history

threat_scenarios.threat: SIM cloning for unauthorized access
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Extract SIM credentials to clone cellular identity

damage_scenarios.description: Vehicle tracking and driver surveillance
Loss of emergency call capability
Unauthorized control of vehicle functions via network pivot
Financial loss from unauthorized service usage
Reputational damage from fleet-wide telemetry breach

cybersecurity_goals.description: Prevent unauthorized remote code execution on TCU
Protect confidentiality of telemetry and location data in transit and at rest
Ensure availability of eCall service under attack conditions
Prevent unauthorized lateral movement from TCU to vehicle bus
Authenticate all firmware updates with cryptographic signature verification

examples.item_definition: The Central Gateway ECU routes messages between heterogeneous in-vehicle networks: CAN, CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, and Automotive Ethernet. It enforces network segmentation by filtering, rate-limiting, and translating frames across domains (powertrain, chassis, body, infotainment, ADAS). The gateway maintains routing tables and firewall rules, performs intrusion detection on cross-domain traffic, and logs anomalous patterns. Trust boundaries exist at each domain interface. The gateway is the single chokepoint for all inter-domain communication, making it a high-value target. It runs an AUTOSAR Classic platform with a secure boot chain.
assets: Routing tables and firewall rule sets

threat_scenarios.threat: Routing table manipulation to bypass domain isolation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit diagnostic session to write malicious routing configuration

threat_scenarios.threat: Flooding attack causing message queue exhaustion
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Inject high-rate CAN frames from compromised ECU to overwhelm gateway buffers

threat_scenarios.threat: Bypassing firewall rules via protocol translation flaws
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Craft frames that exploit CAN-to-Ethernet translation edge cases

threat_scenarios.threat: Secure boot bypass to load modified firmware
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit bootloader vulnerability or fault injection during boot

threat_scenarios.threat: IDS evasion via fragmented or slow-rate attacks
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Send malicious traffic below IDS detection thresholds

threat_scenarios.threat: Extraction of IDS signatures for attack refinement
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Read IDS configuration via diagnostic services

threat_scenarios.threat: Repudiation of gateway log entries
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify or delete intrusion detection logs after compromise

damage_scenarios.description: Loss of domain isolation allowing safety-critical bus access from infotainment
Denial of service on powertrain or chassis bus affecting vehicle control
Undetected persistent compromise of vehicle network
Loss of forensic evidence after security incident

cybersecurity_goals.description: Maintain strict domain isolation between all vehicle network segments
Ensure gateway availability under bus flooding conditions
Protect integrity of routing tables and firewall rules against unauthorized modification
Maintain tamper-evident logging of all cross-domain security events

examples.item_definition: The Diagnostic Interface provides wired (OBD-II/DoCAN) and network-based (DoIP over Ethernet) access for workshop tools, factory end-of-line programming, and field service operations. It implements UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services, ISO 14229) for ECU reprogramming, configuration, fault reading, and routine control. The interface supports multiple security access levels with seed-key authentication per ISO 14229. Trust boundaries exist between the external diagnostic tool, the diagnostic interface module, and the internal vehicle network. The interface can unlock safety-critical ECU functions including calibration writes and actuator control. Physical access to OBD-II port is assumed, but DoIP extends attack surface to workshop LAN.

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized ECU reprogramming via security access bypass
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Reverse-engineer seed-key algorithm from ECU firmware dump

threat_scenarios.threat: Fault memory manipulation to conceal defects
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Use standard UDS ClearDTC service after unauthorized access

threat_scenarios.threat: Remote diagnostic access via DoIP from workshop LAN
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit DoIP gateway from compromised workshop network

threat_scenarios.threat: Actuator control abuse for physical damage
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Invoke RoutineControl services to activate actuators unsafely

threat_scenarios.threat: Extraction of proprietary calibration data
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Use ReadMemoryByAddress to dump ECU intellectual property

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of diagnostic services during safety recall
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Corrupt diagnostic routing or exhaust session limits

damage_scenarios.description: Vehicle malfunction from unauthorized calibration changes
Concealment of safety-relevant defects via fault memory clearing
Theft of proprietary ECU calibration IP
Inability to perform safety recall updates

cybersecurity_goals.description: Enforce multi-level authentication for all diagnostic security access levels
Prevent unauthorized ECU reprogramming and calibration modification
Protect diagnostic fault memory against unauthorized clearing
Restrict DoIP access to authenticated workshop equipment only

examples.item_definition: The Over-the-Air Update Client manages the download, verification, installation, and rollback of firmware and software updates for all ECUs. It communicates with the OEM update backend via TLS, verifies package integrity with cryptographic signatures (ECDSA/RSA), manages A/B partition switching for safe rollback, and coordinates update campaigns across multiple ECUs. Trust boundaries exist between the cloud backend, the TCU relay, the OTA client, and the target ECUs. The system must handle partial update failures gracefully and maintain vehicle operability throughout the update process. Update metadata includes version manifests, dependency graphs, and installation scripts.
assets: Code signing keys (public keys stored locally)
A/B partition state and rollback metadata

threat_scenarios.threat: Installation of unsigned or tampered firmware package
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Man-in-the-middle attack on update download channel or compromised update server

threat_scenarios.threat: Rollback to vulnerable firmware version
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Manipulate version manifest to force installation of known-vulnerable older version

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of critical safety updates
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Block update server connectivity or corrupt download in progress

threat_scenarios.threat: Bricking ECU through interrupted update
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Trigger power loss or communication disruption during flash write

threat_scenarios.threat: Update server impersonation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: DNS hijack or certificate spoofing to redirect update downloads

threat_scenarios.threat: Extraction of proprietary update packages
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Intercept update downloads or extract from storage partition

threat_scenarios.threat: Dependency confusion in multi-ECU update
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify dependency graph to install incompatible firmware combinations

damage_scenarios.description: Fleet-wide malicious firmware deployment
Reintroduction of patched security vulnerabilities across fleet
Vehicles unable to receive critical safety patches
Permanent ECU failure requiring physical workshop repair

cybersecurity_goals.description: Verify cryptographic signature of every update package before installation
Prevent rollback to firmware versions with known vulnerabilities
Maintain vehicle operability during and after any update failure
Authenticate OTA server identity before accepting update packages

examples.item_definition: The Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication module enables direct short-range communication with other vehicles (V2V), infrastructure (V2I), pedestrians (V2P), and network services (V2N). It operates using DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) or C-V2X (3GPP PC5) at 5.9 GHz. Messages include Basic Safety Messages (BSMs) containing vehicle position, speed, heading, and brake status. The module uses a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) for message signing with frequently rotating pseudonym certificates to protect driver privacy. Trust boundaries exist between the wireless interface, the V2X stack, the vehicle fusion layer, and safety-critical actuators that may respond to V2X data. The system must process messages within strict latency requirements (under 100 ms).
assets: PKI root of trust and certificate revocation lists
V2I signal phase and timing information

threat_scenarios.threat: Spoofed BSM injection causing false collision avoidance
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Broadcast fabricated BSMs with false position/speed data

threat_scenarios.threat: Sybil attack creating phantom vehicles
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Use multiple pseudonym certificates simultaneously to simulate traffic congestion

threat_scenarios.threat: Vehicle tracking through pseudonym linkage
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Correlate BSM timing and kinematic data across pseudonym changes

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of V2X safety services via channel jamming
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Flood 5.9 GHz channel to prevent BSM reception

threat_scenarios.threat: Compromise of PKI enrollment authority
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Attack PKI backend to issue unauthorized certificates

threat_scenarios.threat: Replay of old BSMs to cause stale data decisions
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Capture and retransmit valid BSMs after delay

threat_scenarios.threat: Traffic signal spoofing via V2I messages
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Broadcast false Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT) messages

damage_scenarios.description: Unnecessary emergency braking from false collision warnings
Driver privacy violation through long-term vehicle tracking
Loss of cooperative safety functions in critical situation
Traffic disruption from false infrastructure messages

cybersecurity_goals.description: Validate authenticity and plausibility of all received V2X messages
Protect driver privacy through effective pseudonym rotation
Maintain safety function availability under RF jamming conditions
Detect and discard replayed or time-invalid messages
Implement misbehavior detection to identify and report malicious BSM sources

examples.item_definition: The Advanced Driver Assistance Systems controller processes sensor fusion data from cameras, radar, lidar, and ultrasonic sensors to provide functions including adaptive cruise control, lane keeping assist, automatic emergency braking, and parking assist. It runs perception algorithms on a high-performance SoC with GPU/NPU accelerators, consuming data from multiple bus interfaces (Ethernet for cameras, CAN-FD for radar/ultrasonic). The controller outputs actuation commands to steering, braking, and powertrain ECUs. Trust boundaries exist between each sensor input, the fusion processing layer, the decision logic, and the actuation output interfaces. ASIL B to ASIL D requirements apply depending on the function.
assets: Sensor fusion algorithms and ML models
Sensor raw data streams (camera, radar, lidar)

threat_scenarios.threat: Adversarial input causing object misclassification
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Physical adversarial patches on road signs or vehicles to fool camera-based perception

threat_scenarios.threat: Sensor blinding or saturation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Directed laser or electromagnetic interference to blind cameras/radar/lidar

threat_scenarios.threat: Spoofing of radar or lidar returns
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Emit crafted RF or optical signals to create phantom objects or hide real ones

threat_scenarios.threat: Tampering with ML model weights
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify model parameters via compromised update channel or debug access

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized actuation commands via bus injection
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Inject CAN-FD frames mimicking ADAS actuation messages from compromised ECU

threat_scenarios.threat: Map data manipulation causing incorrect path planning
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Tamper with HD map updates to introduce false road geometry

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of ADAS functions via processing overload
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Flood Ethernet interface with high-rate traffic to exhaust processing budget

damage_scenarios.description: Failure to detect pedestrian or obstacle causing collision
Unintended braking or steering maneuver in traffic
Loss of driver assistance in hazardous conditions
Theft of proprietary perception algorithms

cybersecurity_goals.description: Ensure sensor fusion resilience against adversarial and spoofed inputs
Protect ML model integrity against unauthorized modification
Authenticate all actuation commands on the vehicle bus
Maintain safe degradation of ADAS functions under sensor denial conditions
Verify integrity of HD map data before use in path planning

examples.item_definition: The EV Charging Controller manages communication between the electric vehicle and External Charging Supply Equipment (EVSE) using ISO 15118 Plug and Charge protocol. It handles TLS-based mutual authentication, contract certificate management, charging session negotiation (AC/DC power levels, schedules), and payment authorization via Plug and Charge or External Identification Means (EIM). The controller interfaces with the Battery Management System for charge limits and the vehicle network for user interface. Trust boundaries exist between the charging cable (PLC/powerline communication), the charging controller, the vehicle's internal network, and the backend Mobility Operator/Certificate Provisioning Service. The controller stores contract certificates and private keys in a secure element.
assets: Contract certificates and private keys in secure element
Battery state of charge and limit data

threat_scenarios.threat: Billing fraud via charging session manipulation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Tamper with metering data or session parameters to alter billing amounts

threat_scenarios.threat: Man-in-the-middle on PLC communication
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Intercept powerline communication between EV and EVSE to modify charging parameters

threat_scenarios.threat: Extraction of contract certificate private key
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Side-channel attack on secure element during TLS handshake

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized charging using stolen contract identity
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Clone or replay contract certificate for free charging

threat_scenarios.threat: Battery damage through manipulated charging parameters
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Override charge limits to force overcharging or excessive charge rates

threat_scenarios.threat: Grid destabilization via coordinated V2G manipulation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Compromise multiple vehicles to inject/draw power simultaneously in V2G mode

threat_scenarios.threat: Privacy leakage through charging station correlation
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Track vehicle movements by correlating contract certificate usage across charging stations

damage_scenarios.description: Financial loss from billing fraud at scale
Battery thermal event from manipulated charge parameters
Driver privacy violation through charging location tracking
Local grid instability from coordinated V2G attack

cybersecurity_goals.description: Ensure mutual authentication between EV and EVSE using valid contract certificates
Protect integrity of charging session metering and billing data
Enforce BMS-defined charge limits regardless of EVSE parameters
Protect contract certificate private keys against extraction

examples.item_definition: The Keyless Entry System provides passive entry and passive start (PEPS) functionality using low-frequency (125 kHz) wake and ultra-high-frequency (315/433 MHz or UWB) communication between the vehicle and the key fob. The system includes an immobilizer with cryptographic transponder authentication, remote lock/unlock, and in some variants Ultra-Wideband (UWB) distance bounding for relay attack resistance. Trust boundaries exist between the key fob, the RF communication channel, the body control module (BCM) receiver, and the engine immobilizer ECU. The system must authenticate the key fob and verify physical proximity before unlocking doors or enabling engine start. Key pairing data and cryptographic secrets are stored in both the fob and the BCM.
assets: Key fob pairing data and rolling codes
Vehicle unlock and engine start authorization tokens

threat_scenarios.threat: Relay attack extending key fob range
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Use two relay devices to bridge LF/UHF signals between distant fob and vehicle

threat_scenarios.threat: Rolling code prediction or replay
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Capture and analyze rolling codes to predict future valid codes or jam-and-replay

threat_scenarios.threat: Key fob cloning via cryptographic key extraction
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Extract immobilizer secrets from key fob via hardware attack (decapping, fault injection)

threat_scenarios.threat: Jamming of unlock signal causing user frustration
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Transmit interference on 315/433 MHz to prevent legitimate unlock

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized key pairing via diagnostic access
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Use stolen diagnostic tool credentials to pair additional key fobs

threat_scenarios.threat: UWB distance bounding bypass
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit timing inaccuracies in UWB ranging to defeat proximity check

damage_scenarios.description: Vehicle theft via unauthorized entry and engine start
Theft of valuables from unlocked vehicle
Fleet-wide theft vulnerability if cryptographic scheme is broken

cybersecurity_goals.description: Verify physical proximity of key fob using UWB distance bounding
Protect immobilizer cryptographic secrets against extraction
Prevent unauthorized key fob pairing through strong diagnostic authentication
Ensure rolling code scheme resists prediction and replay attacks

examples.item_definition: The Infotainment Head Unit (IVI) provides entertainment, navigation, phone connectivity (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, USB), and vehicle settings interfaces. It runs a Linux-based or Android Automotive OS on an application processor, with connectivity to external devices (smartphones via Apple CarPlay/Android Auto), the internet (via TCU), and the vehicle network (CAN/Ethernet via gateway). Trust boundaries exist between external connectivity interfaces (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, USB), the application runtime environment, the navigation subsystem, and the vehicle network gateway. The IVI processes personal data including contacts, call history, navigation destinations, and media preferences. It represents the largest code surface in the vehicle.
assets: User personal data (contacts, call history, navigation history)
Bluetooth pairing keys and Wi-Fi credentials
Vehicle network access via gateway interface

threat_scenarios.threat: Remote code execution via Bluetooth stack vulnerability
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit known Bluetooth protocol vulnerabilities to gain shell access

threat_scenarios.threat: Malicious USB device exploiting media parser
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Craft USB storage with malformed media files triggering buffer overflow in codec

threat_scenarios.threat: Personal data exfiltration from paired phone data
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Extract synced contacts, messages, and call history after IVI compromise

threat_scenarios.threat: Malicious app installation via compromised app store
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Sideload or push malicious app through compromised update mechanism

threat_scenarios.threat: Pivoting from IVI to vehicle network
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Escape IVI sandbox to send CAN frames through gateway interface

threat_scenarios.threat: Eavesdropping via microphone access
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Activate in-cabin microphone after compromising IVI to record conversations

threat_scenarios.threat: Wi-Fi credential theft and network reconnaissance
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Extract stored Wi-Fi passwords and scan connected networks

damage_scenarios.description: Mass privacy breach from fleet-wide personal data extraction
Vehicle network compromise via IVI as entry point
In-cabin surveillance through microphone/camera access
Driver distraction from IVI malfunction

cybersecurity_goals.description: Isolate IVI application environment from vehicle safety-critical networks
Protect user personal data confidentiality at rest and in transit
Enforce application sandboxing to prevent privilege escalation
Restrict microphone and camera access to authenticated user-initiated sessions only

examples.item_definition: The Battery Management System (BMS) monitors and controls the high-voltage lithium-ion battery pack in electric and hybrid vehicles. It measures cell voltages, temperatures, and current flow, calculates state of charge (SoC), state of health (SoH), and remaining range estimates. The BMS controls cell balancing, thermal management (cooling/heating), and enforces charge/discharge limits to prevent thermal runaway. It communicates with the motor controller, charging controller, and vehicle control unit via CAN-FD. Trust boundaries exist between cell monitoring ICs (slave boards), the BMS master controller, and the vehicle network. The BMS has direct control over high-voltage contactors that connect/disconnect the battery. Safety integrity level is ASIL C to ASIL D.
assets: Cell voltage and temperature sensor data

threat_scenarios.threat: Manipulation of cell voltage readings
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Tamper with SPI communication between slave monitoring ICs and BMS master

threat_scenarios.threat: Override of charge/discharge safety limits
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify BMS calibration via diagnostic access to increase limits beyond safe thresholds

threat_scenarios.threat: Spoofed CAN messages to BMS from motor controller
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Inject CAN-FD frames requesting power beyond safe limits

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of thermal management
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Suppress BMS cooling commands during fast charge or high-load conditions

threat_scenarios.threat: SoH data manipulation for warranty fraud
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify battery health data to falsely report better condition for resale

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized contactor control
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Command high-voltage contactors open during drive or closed during fault condition

damage_scenarios.description: Battery thermal runaway causing fire
Sudden loss of motive power from contactor disconnect
Accelerated battery degradation from unsafe charging
Warranty fraud from falsified battery health records

cybersecurity_goals.description: Protect integrity of cell monitoring data against manipulation
Enforce hardware-backed charge and discharge safety limits independent of software
Authenticate all CAN-FD messages commanding BMS power delivery
Ensure thermal management availability under all operating conditions

examples.item_definition: The Electric Power Steering (EPS) ECU controls the steering assist motor and receives steering angle commands from the ADAS controller and driver steering wheel input. It implements torque overlay functions for lane keeping assist and automated parking. The ECU runs safety-critical software at ASIL D with redundant processing channels (1oo2D architecture) and independent monitoring. Communication occurs via CAN-FD with message authentication (SecOC). Trust boundaries exist between the steering angle sensor, the driver interface, the ADAS torque overlay input, the EPS motor controller, and the redundant safety monitor. Any compromise of steering integrity has direct life-safety consequences.
assets: ADAS torque overlay parameters and limits
SecOC keys and message authentication codes

threat_scenarios.threat: Injection of false steering torque commands
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Spoof CAN-FD messages from ADAS controller with SecOC bypass or key compromise

threat_scenarios.threat: Compromise of steering assist calibration
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Modify EPS calibration parameters via diagnostic access to alter steering feel and limits

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of power steering assist
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Trigger safety shutdown by injecting conflicting sensor data

threat_scenarios.threat: SecOC key extraction for message authentication bypass
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Extract SecOC keys from ECU firmware or memory via debug interface

threat_scenarios.threat: Bypassing redundant safety monitor
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Simultaneously compromise both processing channels to suppress safety reactions

damage_scenarios.description: Sudden unintended steering causing road departure or collision
Loss of power steering assist at speed
Fleet-wide steering vulnerability if SecOC keys compromised

cybersecurity_goals.description: Authenticate all steering torque commands using SecOC with fresh keys
Protect steering calibration parameters against unauthorized modification
Maintain independent safety monitor integrity separate from main processing
Enforce torque overlay limits that cannot be exceeded by software commands

examples.item_definition: The Fleet Management Backend is a cloud-hosted service that aggregates telemetry from thousands of connected vehicles, provides fleet operators with real-time dashboards, geofencing, driver behavior analytics, and remote vehicle commands (lock, unlock, immobilize, honk, flash). It consists of an API gateway, message broker (MQTT/AMQP), time-series database, command dispatcher, and web dashboard. Trust boundaries exist between the public internet, the API gateway, the internal microservices, the message broker, the vehicle communication channel (via TCU), and the operator dashboard. The backend stores sensitive data including all vehicle locations, driver identities, and vehicle command authorization tokens. It must enforce multi-tenancy to prevent cross-fleet data access.
assets: Vehicle telemetry data (GPS, speed, fuel, diagnostics)
Fleet operator credentials and API keys

threat_scenarios.threat: Unauthorized remote vehicle command execution
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit API authorization flaw to send immobilize/unlock commands to arbitrary vehicles

threat_scenarios.threat: Cross-tenant data access in multi-fleet environment
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: IDOR or broken access control allowing fleet operator to view other tenants' vehicles

threat_scenarios.threat: Mass vehicle tracking through API data exfiltration
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Compromise operator credentials to bulk-download GPS history

threat_scenarios.threat: Message broker poisoning with false telemetry
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Inject fake MQTT messages to create false vehicle positions or statuses

threat_scenarios.threat: Denial of fleet management services
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: DDoS attack on API gateway or message broker exhaustion

threat_scenarios.threat: Privilege escalation from operator to admin role
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Exploit role management API to elevate permissions

threat_scenarios.threat: Supply chain attack on backend dependencies
threat_scenarios.attack_vector: Compromise npm/pip package used by backend microservices

damage_scenarios.description: Remote immobilization of vehicles in traffic causing accidents
Mass vehicle theft through unauthorized fleet unlock
GDPR privacy breach from bulk location data exfiltration
Fleet operational disruption from backend unavailability

cybersecurity_goals.description: Enforce strict multi-tenant data isolation with zero cross-fleet access
Require multi-factor authorization for all safety-critical remote vehicle commands
Authenticate and validate all inbound vehicle telemetry messages
Maintain audit trail of all remote commands with operator attribution
Protect driver location data with encryption and access controls per GDPR
